text: This video presents a wide local excision robotic prostatectomy for high-risk prostate cancer.

The video shows the wide local excision during robotic prostatectomy, highlighting the intact non-VA fascia with visible perirectal fat. The extended lymph node dissection encompasses nodal tissue between the internal and external iliac vessels, surrounding the obturator nerve, and extending proximally to the common iliac bifurcation. The procedure includes a wide posterior lateral prostatic margin and a wide bladder neck excision and reconstruction.
Here are MRIs of patients in whom we've performed wide local excision. This MRI shows high burden disease with left extracapsular extension. This MRI shows left seminal vesicle involvement.
We've performed approximately twenty wide local excisions, and what follows is a representative video of a few.
The surgeon uses the posterior approach to make an incision along the posterior cul-de-sac during the robotic prostatectomy procedure.

The surgeon identifies the non-vious fascia and dissects directly down to the longitudinal fibers of the rectum, bypassing the typical dissection along the seminal vesicle and vas deferens to the base of the prostate.
The surgeon carefully skeletonizes the lateral aspects of the prostate, staying close to the rectum, and exposes the endopelvic fascia.
The surgeon has completed dissection down to the levator ani muscles on both the left and right sides, revealing these muscles in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the seminal vesicles and vas deferens, carefully separating these structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the lateral aspect of the vas deferens and transects it sharply, using the cut end to provide traction for further manipulation.
The surgeon identifies the lateral pedicle of the seminal vesicle, applies clips to secure it, and then transects the pedicle.
The surgeon dissects anteriorly along the seminal vesicle, stopping when the detrusor fibers become visible, ensuring good tissue coverage of the seminal vesicles without reaching the bladder neck.
The surgeon begins the pelvic lymph node dissection, taking advantage of the clear visibility maintained after the prostate resection.

The surgeon extends the pelvic lymph node dissection to include nodal tissue between the internal and external iliac vessels, surrounding the obturator nerve, and up to the common iliac bifurcation. The lateral edge of the rectum is visible, and all nodal tissue has been removed up to the common iliac bifurcation.
The surgeon uses a standard stitch to ligate the dorsal venous complex, followed by a wide incision at the bladder neck to enhance visualization of the ureteral orifices.
With T-for disease, we have the option of reimplanting the ureters after reconstructing the bladder neck.

The surgeon leaves the lateral prostatic pedicles intact during bladder neck reconstruction, using the third arm of the surgical robot to lift the prostate and rotate the bladder neck into view for better exposure.
During the bladder neck reconstruction, the surgeon clearly visualizes the left and right ureter orifices, ensuring precise anatomical orientation.

The surgeon maintains a wide posterolateral margin at the prostatic pedicles, ensuring clear margins in areas prone to positive surgical margins in high-grade disease. The contralateral side is approached in a nerve-sparing manner when indicated.

The surgeon has completed the dissection of periprostatic tissue, removing it along with the prostate. The apical dissection is performed using standard techniques, with clear visualization of the prostate's apex.
The surgeon performs the bladder anastomosis using a double-armed V-lock suture, following standard technique to connect the bladder neck to the urethra.

The final specimen displays a wide bladder neck excision and reconstruction, with wide posterior lateral prostatic margins and intact fascia. The specimen includes all perirectal fat and an extended lymph node dissection, suitable for patients with high-risk disease.
